Your adjustable clamp ↻
drag to spin
The Digital World in 3D · Lab 7A

Design the Adjustable
Desk Hook in Fusion 360

Lab 6’s hook gripped one desk — your desk, 19.0 mm exactly. Project six has a bigger brief: clamp any desk from 12 to 32 mm. Fixed jaws can’t do that. Screws can. You’ll design your first screw from theory up — pitch, thread depth, handedness, printable profiles — cut the matching nut straight into the jaw, then print the two pieces separately, thread them together by hand, and clamp four different desks without reprinting a thing. Six lessons.

Units & spelling
what the symbols mean — Ø, R, pitch, +0.4, ″…
mmmillimetres — every number in this course. 1 mm ≈ a bank card’s thickness; 0.1 mm ≈ a human hair.in / ″inches — the imperial unit: 1 ″ = exactly 25.4 mm. Working in inches? Flip the units: mm pill in the top bar and every dimension gains its inch twin, like 44 mm (1.73″).Ø10.4diameter — the width of a circle measured straight across, through its centre: 10.4 mm across. Engineers say “dia ten-point-four”. (A slashed circle — not a zero, not the letter O.)R2radius — centre to edge: half the diameter, so R2 is the same circle as Ø4. Fillets are given as radii.pitch 2.5the gap between neighbouring turns of a thread, in mm. One full turn of the screw travels exactly one pitch — 2.5 mm here.+0.4clearance — how much bigger we make the nut than its screw. Ø10 thread in a Ø10.4 nut leaves 0.4 mm of air: exact jams, exact + clearance turns.45°degrees of angle — 360° is one full spin. 45° is the chamfer angle a printer can build without support.×12times — the screw’s force multiplier: push 31.4 mm around the thread to climb 2.5 mm up it, and your grip is magnified ≈12×.πpi ≈ 3.14 — the distance around any circle ÷ its diameter. It sets the ramp length when you unroll one turn of thread.
Tip: stuck or away last lesson? Your progress is saved, and every step has a “Show me ▶” button.

Fusion 360 · Interactive Lab

Lesson 1 — The Arm

A 53 × 6 mm arm that rides on top of the desk — drawn on a VERTICAL sketch plane for the first time.

The Open C-Frame

Ø?
mmmillimetres — every number in this course. 1 mm ≈ a bank card’s thickness; 0.1 mm ≈ a human hair.in / ″inches — the imperial unit: 1 ″ = exactly 25.4 mm. Working in inches? Flip the units: mm pill in the top bar and every dimension gains its inch twin, like 44 mm (1.73″).Ø10.4diameter — the width of a circle measured straight across, through its centre: 10.4 mm across. Engineers say “dia ten-point-four”. (A slashed circle — not a zero, not the letter O.)R2radius — centre to edge: half the diameter, so R2 is the same circle as Ø4. Fillets are given as radii.pitch 2.5the gap between neighbouring turns of a thread, in mm. One full turn of the screw travels exactly one pitch — 2.5 mm here.+0.4clearance — how much bigger we make the nut than its screw. Ø10 thread in a Ø10.4 nut leaves 0.4 mm of air: exact jams, exact + clearance turns.45°degrees of angle — 360° is one full spin. 45° is the chamfer angle a printer can build without support.×12times — the screw’s force multiplier: push 31.4 mm around the thread to climb 2.5 mm up it, and your grip is magnified ≈12×.πpi ≈ 3.14 — the distance around any circle ÷ its diameter. It sets the ramp length when you unroll one turn of thread.
